DMSTAr 0.1.1
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Getting Started with DMSTAr
    • k-HLR relationships
    • Phosphorus Kinetics in DMSTAr
  • Changelog

k-HLR relationships

Source: vignettes/kHLR_relationships.Rmd
kHLR_relationships.Rmd

The purpose of this vignette is to explain a way to derive “equivalent”k-HLR relationships that let you translate between Dynamic DMSTA-style P model parameters (ie. K1K_1, K2K_2, K3K_3,C0C_0 and C1C_1), and the familiar k−C*k-C^*/N-tank-in-series design relationships used for screening and sizing of treatment wetlands.

DMSTA was conceptualized as a dynamic extension of a steady state settling rate model. In practice, DMSTA maintains dynamic water balance, hydraulic routing and P cycling routines. Generally, P removal is proportional to the product of water column P and available P storage in vegetation and sediment. Meanwhile, P recycling is described mathematically as a quadratic to the liable pool. Finally P removal to permanent burial is 1st order from labile storage.

DMSTA has three key rate constants, K1K_1, K2K_2 and K3K_3 that scale P biogeochemistry represented by the model (removal, recycle and burial). These are the principal fitting parameters for calibration to observed data. These parameters were linearly transformed to a different parameter space from KK, C0C_0 and C1C_1.

  • KK = net settling rate at steady state (m/yr)
  • C0C_0 = water column concentration at zero labile storage (μgL−1\mu g \ L^{-1})
  • C1C_1 = water column concentration when labile storage is at 1 gm−2g \ m^{-2} (μgL−1\mu g \ L^{-1})

Typically, KK is estimated by fitting a model to a concentration time-series, C0C_0 and C1C_1 are estimated from the storage and concentration relationship for a given wetlands and/or vegetative community.


The Models:

  1. The NKC*NKC^* Model: A generalized tank-in-series outflow model represented as
Cout=Cin−C*(1+kN⋅HLR)N+C*C_{out} = \frac{C_{in} - C^{*}}{(1 + \frac{k}{N \cdot HLR})^{N}} + C^*
Model variables
Variable Definition Units
QQ Flow m3y−1\mathrm{m^{3}\ y^{-1}}
AA Area m2\mathrm{m^{2}}
HLRHLR Hydraulic Looading Rate HLR=Q/AHLR = Q/A my−1\mathrm{m\ y^{-1}}
CinC_{in},CoutC_{out} Inflow/Outflow Concentration mgm−3orμgL−1\mathrm{mg\ m^{-3}\ or \ \mu g\ L^{-1}}
C*C^* Background concentration mgm−3orμgL−1\mathrm{mg\ m^{-3}\ or \ \mu g\ L^{-1}}
kk areal net settling (removal velocity) my−1\mathrm{m\ y^{-1}}
NN Number of tanks in series unitless

When N=1N = 1 it indicates a fully mixed tank (conservative), meanwhile N=3−5N = 3 - 5 is the common STA design range.

Using equation above, here are some useful limited cases and/or simplifications of the model.

  • N=1N = 1 (single Continuously Stirred Tank Reactor [CSTR])

Cout=C*+Cin−C*1+kN⋅HLRC_{out} = C^{*} + \frac{C_{in} - C^{*}}{1 + \frac{k}{N \cdot HLR}}

  • N→∞N \to\infty (plug-flow limit)

Cout=C*+(Cin−C*)e−k/HLRC_{out} = C^{*} + (C_{in} - C^{*})e^{-k / HLR}


Using the tank in series equation, here is the inverse relationship


Solve for required HLR (given target CoutC_{out})

Rearrange the NKC*NKC^* formula:

(1+kN⋅HLR)N=Cin−C*Cout−C*\bigg( 1 + \frac{k}{N \cdot HLR} \bigg)^N = \frac{C_{in} - C^*}{C_{out} - C^{*}} HLR=k/N(Cin−C*Cout−C*)1/N−1HLR = \frac{k/N}{\big( \frac{C_{in} - C^*}{C_{out} - C^{*}} \big)^{1/N} -1} Then area for a specified flow QQ is:

A=QHLRA = \frac{Q}{HLR}Domain constraints: you need Cout>C*C_{out} > C^{*} and Cin>C*C_{in} > C^{*}. If Cout≤C*C_{out} \le C^{*}, the outflow is exceeding the assumed background concentration.


Solve for k (given observed CinC_{in},CoutC_{out} and HLRHLR)

k=N⋅HLR[(Cin−C*Cout−C*)1/N−1]k = N \cdot HLR \bigg[ \bigg( \frac{C_{in} - C^{*}}{C_{out} - C^{*}} \bigg)^{1/N} -1 \bigg] This is the equivalent settling velocity (myr−1m \ yr^{-1}) implied by observed performance (at the chosen NN and $C^{8}).

  • N=1N = 1 (single Continuously Stirred Tank Reactor [CSTR])

k=HLR⋅(Cin−C*Cout−C*−1)k = HLR \cdot \bigg( \frac{C_{in}-C^{*}}{C_{out}-C^{*}} -1 \bigg)

  • N→∞N \to\infty (plug-flow limit)

k=HLR⋅ln(Cin−C*Cout−C*)k = HLR \cdot ln\bigg( \frac{C_{in}-C^{*}}{C_{out}-C^{*}} \bigg)

This is often the method to estimate the upper bounds of kk from observed annual data.


  1. DMSTA Model: Conceptually represented as

DMSTA State Variables and Parameters
Symbol Description Units
State Variables
M Water column P storage mgm−2\mathrm{mg\ m^{-2}}
S Biomass P storage mgm−2\mathrm{mg\ m^{-2}}
Z Mean water depth mm
Driving Variables
L External P load mgm−2yr−1\mathrm{mg\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}}
Q Outflow rate myr−1\mathrm{m\ yr^{-1}}
Parameter Variables
K1 Maximum uptake rate m3mg−1yr−1\mathrm{m^{3}\ mg^{-1}\ yr^{-1}}
K2 Recycle rate m2mg−1yr−1\mathrm{m^{2}\ mg^{-1}\ yr^{-1}}
K3 Burial rate yr−1\mathrm{yr^{-1}}
Steady-State Mass Balances 1 CSTR & Fz = 1
Compartment Equation
Storage: K1⋅C=K2⋅S+K3K1 \cdot C = K2 \cdot S + K3
Overall: L−Q⋅C=K3⋅SL - Q \cdot C = K3 \cdot S
Solution for C: C=(K2⋅L+K32)K3⋅K1+Q⋅K2C = \frac{(K2 \cdot L + K3^{2})}{K3 \cdot K1 + Q \cdot K2}
Solution for S: S=(K1⋅C−K3)K2S = \frac{(K1 \cdot C - K3)}{K2}

Whereas the steady-state DMSTA solution is equivalent to a simple KC*KC^{*} model with C*=C0C^{*} = C_0 and KK is interpreted as a net settling rate (myr−1m \ yr^{-1}). Therefore, for “equivalent design model” purposes C*≡C0C^{*} \equiv C_{0} and k≡Kk \equiv K. This means that the DMSTAs calibrated (KK and C0C_{0}) can be subsituted directly into the NKC*NKC^{*} model.

Where C=(L+K⋅C0)(K+Q)C = \frac{(L + K \cdot C_{0})}{(K + Q)}

S=1000⋅C−C0C1−C0S = 1000 \cdot \frac{C-C_0}{C1 - C_0}

Where,

K=K2⋅K1K2K = \frac{K2 \cdot K1}{K2}

Therefore, given (KK,C0C_0,C1C_1), (K1K1, K2K2, K3K3) can be estimated:

K3=K⋅C1−C01000K3 = K \cdot \frac{C_{1} - C_{0}}{1000}

K1=K3C0K1 = \frac{K3}{C_0}

K2=K3⋅K1KK_2 = \frac{K3 \cdot K1}{K}

Given (K1K1, K2K2, K3K3), (KK,C0C_0,C1C_1) can be estimated:

C0=K3K1C_{0} = \frac{K_3}{K_1}

K=K1⋅K3K2K = \frac{K_1 \cdot K_3}{K_2}

C1=1000⋅K2+K3K1 C_1 = \frac{1000 \cdot K_{2} + K_{3}}{K_{1}}

Helpful R Code

To see these equation in practice here are some simple R functions and examples.

# Forward NKC* (tanks-in-series) prediction
nkc_out <- function(Cin, k, HLR, N = 1, Cstar) {
  Cstar + (Cin - Cstar) / (1 + k / (N * HLR))^N
}

# Solve for HLR given target Cout
nkc_hlr <- function(Cin, Cout, k, N = 1, Cstar) {
  r <- (Cin - Cstar) / (Cout - Cstar)
  (k / N) / (r^(1/N) - 1)
}

# Solve for k given observed Cin/Cout and HLR
nkc_k <- function(Cin, Cout, HLR, N = 1, Cstar) {
  if (any(Cout <= Cstar))
    stop("Cout must be greater than Cstar")
  
  r <- (Cin - Cstar) / (Cout - Cstar)
  N * HLR * (r^(1/N) - 1)
}

# DMSTA parameter translations 
dmsta_KC_to_K123 <- function(K, C0, C1) {
  K3 <- K * (C1 - C0) / 1000
  K1 <- K3 / C0
  K2 <- K3 * K1 / K
  list(K1 = K1, K2 = K2, K3 = K3)
}

dmsta_K123_to_KC <- function(K1, K2, K3) {
  C0 <- K3 / K1
  K  <- (K1 * K3) / K2
  C1 <- (1000 * K2 + K3) / K1
  list(K = K, C0 = C0, C1 = C1)
}

# Estimate k from plug and flow
estimate_k_plug <- function(
  Cin, Cout,
  HLR,
  Cstar
) {
  if (any(Cout <= Cstar))
    stop("Cout must be greater than Cstar")

  HLR * log( (Cin - Cstar) / (Cout - Cstar) )
}
## Example Data
Cin <-  122
Cout <- 17
k  <-  66.5
HLR <- 9.5
Cstar <- 2

K <- 16.8
C0 <- 2
C1 <- 22

K1 <- 0.168
K2 <- 0.00336
K3 <- 0.336

# Estimated Cout given Cin, k, & HLR
nkc_out(Cin, k, HLR, N = 1, Cstar)
#> [1] 17

# Estimated HLR given Cin, Cout & k
nkc_hlr(Cin,Cout,k,N = 1,Cstar)
#> [1] 9.5

# vs more tanks
nkc_hlr(Cin,Cout,k,N = 3,Cstar)
#> [1] 22.16667

## Estimate k 
nkc_k(Cin,Cout,HLR,1,Cstar)
#> [1] 66.5

# estimate model parameters
dmsta_KC_to_K123(K,C0,C1)
#> $K1
#> [1] 0.168
#> 
#> $K2
#> [1] 0.00336
#> 
#> $K3
#> [1] 0.336

dmsta_K123_to_KC(K1,K2,K3)
#> $K
#> [1] 16.8
#> 
#> $C0
#> [1] 2
#> 
#> $C1
#> [1] 22
